> **File Path:** `/root/Documents/Oscar GC/Commercial/S32-821314 Sprint 30 — Pemberton RFQ pack + redline c09/03-pricing-schedule.docx`

# Pricing Schedule

Pemberton Engineering Ltd. — Indirect MRO Framework — 3-Year Term

**Document 03 of 7 — to be completed by the Bidder and returned with bid**

**Pemberton ref:** PEL/PROC/2026-RFQ-073 **Issued:** 11 May 2026

This Pricing Schedule forms part of the Master Supply Agreement (Document 02). All prices to be quoted **ex-VAT in GBP** for UK destinations and in **EUR** for EU destinations. Bidders must complete every line; “POA” or “TBC” responses will result in disqualification.

# 1. Category Scope

The framework covers the following indirect MRO categories:

Cat | Category | Indicative Annual Spend (£)
A | Cutting tools and abrasives | 4,200,000
B | Industrial fasteners | 2,800,000
C | Lubricants and process fluids | 3,400,000
D | PPE and workwear | 1,900,000
E | Electrical components and motors | 3,100,000
F | Pneumatics and hydraulics | 2,000,000
G | Capital spares (nominated SKU list) | 1,000,000
 | **Total indicative annual spend** | **18,400,000**

Spend figures are based on the twelve months ending 31 March 2026 and are indicative only. No commitment is made to any minimum volume.

# 2. Ceiling Prices

For each SKU in Appendix A (the SKU master list — 1,247 SKUs), bidders must quote a unit ceiling price. The ceiling price represents the maximum the Bidder may charge during the first twelve (12) months of the Term, irrespective of actual market price movements.

# 3. Rebate Ladder

Bidders shall propose a rebate structure on the following ladder. Rebates accrue on aggregated group spend (across all UK and EU sites) per calendar quarter and are paid in arrears within thirty (30) days of quarter end by credit note.

Tier | Quarterly aggregate spend (£) | Min. rebate %
1 | Up to 750,000 | 0.5%
2 | 750,001 – 1,500,000 | 1.5%
3 | 1,500,001 – 2,500,000 | 2.5%
4 | Above 2,500,000 | 4.0%

Bidders may propose higher rebate percentages or additional tiers in their bid response.

# 4. Volume Discount Triggers

Quarterly volume discounts above the rebate ladder apply automatically on the following category-level triggers:

* Category A spend exceeds £1.2m in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category A

* Category C spend exceeds £1.0m in a quarter: additional 0.75% on Category C

* Category E spend exceeds £900k in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category E

# 5. Currency and Payment

5.1 UK invoices in GBP. EU invoices in EUR at the local site’s billing entity.

5.2 Foreign exchange risk is borne by the Supplier. No FX-related price adjustments are permitted within the first twelve (12) months.

5.3 Payment terms per MSA clause 6 (90 days from receipt of valid invoice).

# 6. Open-Book Costing for Top-50 SKUs

For the fifty (50) highest-spend SKUs as identified by Buyer from time to time, Supplier shall provide open-book costing comprising landed cost, Supplier margin, and any rebates received from upstream manufacturers. Buyer reserves the right to require margin compression where Supplier margin exceeds twelve percent (12%) on any open-book SKU.

# 7. Most-Favoured-Customer

Supplier represents that, for substantially comparable Goods supplied on substantially comparable terms, the prices quoted under this Pricing Schedule are no less favourable than those offered to any other customer of Supplier. Supplier shall promptly notify Buyer of any improvement in pricing offered to a third party and shall extend such improvement to Buyer.

# 8. Pricing Schedule Return Format

Bidders shall return Appendix A as an Excel workbook with the columns set out in the template. PDF returns will not be accepted. Bidders must also provide:

* A written justification for any SKU where the quoted ceiling price exceeds Buyer’s internal benchmark by more than twenty percent (20%) (Buyer will share benchmark deltas during the clarification window with shortlisted bidders);

* An explanation of methodology for any FX, freight, or duty assumptions baked into landed cost;

* A statement of any sustainability premia included in pricing (e.g. low-carbon steel, recycled-content fasteners).

# Appendix A — SKU Master List (Extract)

SKU | Description | UoM | Indicative annual qty | Bid ceiling price (£)
A-0001 | HSS twist drill bit, 6mm | each | 12,400 | __________
A-0002 | Carbide insert, CCMT 09T308 | each | 38,200 | __________
A-0103 | Diamond abrasive disc, 230mm | each | 6,900 | __________
B-0044 | M12 hex bolt, A2-70, 60mm | each | 184,000 | __________
C-0017 | ISO VG 68 hydraulic oil | 208L drum | 412 | __________
D-0202 | Cut-resistant glove level D, size L | pair | 24,800 | __________
E-0091 | 3-phase induction motor 5.5kW IE3 | each | 92 | __________
F-0028 | Pneumatic cylinder ISO 15552 80x200 | each | 148 | __________
G-0007 | Bearing 6308-2RS | each | 2,420 | __________

_[Full SKU master list of 1,247 lines provided as separate Excel workbook on portal — extract above is illustrative.]_

# 4. Volume Discount Triggers

Quarterly volume discounts above the rebate ladder apply automatically on the following category-level triggers:

* Category A spend exceeds £1.2m in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category A

* Category C spend exceeds £1.0m in a quarter: additional 0.75% on Category C

* Category E spend exceeds £900k in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category E

# 5. Currency and Payment

5.1 UK invoices in GBP. EU invoices in EUR at the local site’s billing entity.

5.2 Foreign exchange risk is borne by the Supplier. No FX-related price adjustments are permitted within the first twelve (12) months.

5.3 Payment terms per MSA clause 6 (90 days from receipt of valid invoice).

# 6. Open-Book Costing for Top-50 SKUs

For the fifty (50) highest-spend SKUs as identified by Buyer from time to time, Supplier shall provide open-book costing comprising landed cost, Supplier margin, and any rebates received from upstream manufacturers. Buyer reserves the right to require margin compression where Supplier margin exceeds twelve percent (12%) on any open-book SKU.

# 7. Most-Favoured-Customer

Supplier represents that, for substantially comparable Goods supplied on substantially comparable terms, the prices quoted under this Pricing Schedule are no less favourable than those offered to any other customer of Supplier. Supplier shall promptly notify Buyer of any improvement in pricing offered to a third party and shall extend such improvement to Buyer.

# 8. Pricing Schedule Return Format

Bidders shall return Appendix A as an Excel workbook with the columns set out in the template. PDF returns will not be accepted. Bidders must also provide:

* A written justification for any SKU where the quoted ceiling price exceeds Buyer’s internal benchmark by more than twenty percent (20%) (Buyer will share benchmark deltas during the clarification window with shortlisted bidders);

* An explanation of methodology for any FX, freight, or duty assumptions baked into landed cost;

* A statement of any sustainability premia included in pricing (e.g. low-carbon steel, recycled-content fasteners).

# Appendix A — SKU Master List (Extract)

SKU | Description | UoM | Indicative annual qty | Bid ceiling price (£)

C-0017 | ISO VG 68 hydraulic oil | 208L drum | 412 | __________
D-0202 | Cut-resistant glove level D, size L | pair | 24,800 | __________

_[Full SKU master list of 1,247 lines provided as separate Excel workbook on portal — extract above is illustrative.]_

<!-- FILE: src/adeu/templates/markdown_ui.html -->
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

<head>
    <meta charset="utf-8">
    <title>Adeu Markdown UI</title>
    <meta name="color-scheme" content="light dark">
    <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
    <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
    <link
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Jost:wght@400;500;600&family=Lora:ital,wght@0,600;1,600&display=swap"
        rel="stylesheet">
    <style>
        :root {
            --brand-sand: #e8c0a1;
            --brand-sand-light: #f2d9c7;
            --brand-sand-dark: #d4a882;
            --brand-mint: #e6f3f0;
            --brand-mint-dark: #c9e4de;
            --brand-navy: #1a2a2c;
            --brand-navy-light: #2a3a3c;
            --brand-mauve: #dacad2;
            --neutral-50: #fafafa;
            --neutral-100: #f5f5f5;
            --neutral-200: #e5e5e5;
            --neutral-300: #d9d9d9;
            --neutral-600: #555969;
            --neutral-800: #1e2028;
            --neutral-900: #171717;
            --font-lora: 'Lora', Georgia, serif;
            --font-jost: 'Jost', system-ui, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, sans-serif;
            --bg-body: var(--neutral-50);
            --bg-card: #ffffff;
            --border-card: var(--neutral-200);
            --text-main: var(--neutral-600);
            --text-heading: var(--brand-navy);
            --bg-blockquote: rgba(232, 192, 161, 0.15);
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
            :root {
                --bg-body: var(--neutral-900);
                --bg-card: var(--brand-navy);
                --border-card: var(--brand-navy-light);
                --text-main: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.85);
                --text-heading: #ffffff;
                --bg-blockquote: rgba(232, 192, 161, 0.1);
                --neutral-100: var(--brand-navy-light);
            }
        }

        html,
        body {
            margin: 0;
            padding: 0;
            box-sizing: border-box;
            background-color: transparent;
        }

        body {
            font-family: var(--font-jost);
            color: var(--text-main);
            line-height: 1.6;
            -webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
            padding: 16px;
        }

        .adeu-card {
            background-color: var(--bg-card);
            border: 1px solid var(--border-card);
            border-radius: 12px;
            overflow: hidden;
            display: flex;
            flex-direction: column;
        }

        .navbar {
            flex-shrink: 0;
            background-color: var(--bg-card);
            padding: 16px 32px;
            border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-card);
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            justify-content: space-between;
            position: relative;
        }

        .logo-container {
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            flex: 1;
        }

        .nav-title {
            position: absolute;
            left: 50%;
            transform: translateX(-50%);
            font-family: var(--font-lora);
            font-weight: 600;
            color: var(--text-heading);
            font-size: 1.1rem;
            white-space: nowrap;
            overflow: hidden;
            text-overflow: ellipsis;
            max-width: 40%;
            text-align: center;
        }

        .nav-actions {
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            justify-content: flex-end;
            flex: 1;
        }

        #copy-btn {
            display: none;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 6px;
            background: transparent;
            border: none;
            color: var(--text-main);
            font-family: var(--font-jost);
            font-size: 0.85rem;
            font-weight: 500;
            cursor: pointer;
            padding: 8px;
            border-radius: 6px;
            transition: all 0.2s ease;
        }

        #copy-btn:hover {
            color: var(--text-heading);
            background: var(--neutral-100);
        }

        #open-btn {
            display: none;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 6px;
            background: transparent;
            border: none;
            color: var(--text-main);
            font-family: var(--font-jost);
            font-size: 0.85rem;
            font-weight: 500;
            cursor: pointer;
            padding: 8px;
            border-radius: 6px;
            transition: all 0.2s ease;
        }

        #open-btn:hover {
            color: var(--text-heading);
            background: var(--neutral-100);
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
            #copy-btn:hover {
                background: var(--brand-navy-light);
            }
            #open-btn:hover {
                background: var(--brand-navy-light);
            }
        }

        .logo-container svg {
            max-width: 80px;
            height: auto;
        }

        .logo-container svg path,
        .logo-container svg rect,
        .logo-container svg polygon,
        .logo-container svg circle {
            transition: fill 0.2s ease;
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {

            .logo-container svg path,
            .logo-container svg rect,
            .logo-container svg polygon,
            .logo-container svg circle,
            .logo-container svg {
                fill: #ffffff !important;
            }
        }

        .content-wrapper {
            position: relative;
        }

        .content-wrapper::-webkit-scrollbar {
            width: 6px;
        }

        .content-wrapper::-webkit-scrollbar-track {
            background: transparent;
        }

        .content-wrapper::-webkit-scrollbar-thumb {
            background-color: var(--border-card);
            border-radius: 4px;
        }

        #app {
            padding: 32px;
            display: flow-root;
        }

        .fade-overlay {
            position: absolute;
            bottom: 0;
            left: 0;
            right: 0;
            height: 80px;
            background: linear-gradient(to bottom, rgba(255, 255, 255, 0), var(--bg-card));
            pointer-events: none;
            display: none;
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
            .fade-overlay {
                background: linear-gradient(to bottom, rgba(26, 42, 44, 0), var(--bg-card));
            }
        }

        .toggle-container {
            flex-shrink: 0;
            display: none;
            justify-content: center;
            align-items: center;
            padding: 12px;
            border-top: 1px solid var(--border-card);
            background-color: var(--bg-card);
        }

        #toggle-btn {
            background: transparent;
            border: none;
            color: var(--brand-sand-dark);
            font-family: var(--font-jost);
            font-weight: 600;
            font-size: 0.95rem;
            cursor: pointer;
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 6px;
            padding: 8px 16px;
            border-radius: 6px;
            transition: all 0.2s ease;
        }

        #toggle-btn:hover {
            color: var(--text-heading);
            background: var(--neutral-100);
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
            #toggle-btn:hover {
                background: var(--brand-navy-light);
            }
        }

        #toggle-icon {
            transition: transform 0.3s ease;
        }

        h1,
        h2,
        h3,
        h4 {
            font-family: var(--font-lora);
            color: var(--text-heading);
            font-weight: 600;
            letter-spacing: -0.02em;
            margin-top: 1.5em;
            margin-bottom: 0.5em;
        }

        h1 {
            font-size: 2.25rem;
            border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border-card);
            padding-bottom: 0.3em;
            margin-top: 0;
        }

        h2 {
            font-size: 1.75rem;
        }

        h3 {
            font-size: 1.25rem;
        }

        a {
            color: var(--brand-sand-dark);
            text-decoration: none;
            border-bottom: 1px solid transparent;
            transition: border 0.2s ease;
        }

        a:hover {
            border-bottom-color: var(--brand-sand-dark);
        }

        strong {
            font-weight: 600;
            color: var(--text-heading);
        }

        ul,
        ol {
            padding-left: 1.5rem;
            margin-bottom: 1rem;
        }

        li {
            margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
        }

        blockquote {
            border-left: 4px solid var(--brand-sand);
            padding: 12px 20px;
            margin: 24px 0;
            color: var(--text-main);
            background: var(--bg-blockquote);
            border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
            font-style: italic;
        }

        code {
            font-family: ui-monospace, SFMono-Regular, Consolas, "Liberation Mono", Menlo, monospace;
            font-size: 0.85em;
            background: var(--neutral-100);
            color: var(--brand-sand-dark);
            padding: 0.2em 0.4em;
            border-radius: 4px;
        }

        pre code {
            display: block;
            padding: 16px;
            background: var(--neutral-900);
            color: #e5e5e5;
            border-radius: 8px;
            overflow-x: auto;
        }

        .skeleton {
            background: linear-gradient(90deg, var(--neutral-100) 25%, var(--border-card) 50%, var(--neutral-100) 75%);
            background-size: 200% 100%;
            animation: skeleton 1.5s ease-in-out infinite;
            border-radius: 4px;
        }

        @keyframes skeleton {
            0% {
                background-position: 200% 0;
            }

            100% {
                background-position: -200% 0;
            }
        }

        .alert {
            padding: 16px;
            border-radius: 8px;
            margin-bottom: 20px;
            display: flex;
            flex-direction: column;
            gap: 8px;
            background-color: #fef2f2;
            border: 1px solid #fecaca;
            color: #991b1b;
        }

        @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) {
            .alert {
                background-color: rgba(127, 29, 29, 0.2);
                border-color: rgba(248, 113, 113, 0.3);
                color: #fca5a5;
            }
        }

        .alert-title {
            font-weight: 600;
            font-size: 1.1em;
            margin: 0;
            display: flex;
            align-items: center;
            gap: 8px;
            font-family: var(--font-lora);
        }

        .alert-body {
            margin: 0;
            font-size: 0.95em;
        }

        .alert-details {
            background: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.05);
            padding: 12px;
            border-radius: 6px;
            font-family: ui-monospace, Consolas, monospace;
            font-size: 0.85em;
            white-space: pre-wrap;
            word-break: break-all;
            margin-top: 8px;
            border: 1px solid rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.1);
        }
    </style>

</svg>

            </div>

            <div class="nav-title" id="nav-title"></div>

            <div class="nav-actions">
                <button id="copy-btn" title="Copy Markdown">
                    <svg id="copy-icon" width="18" height="18" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"
                        stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round">
                        <rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2" ry="2"></rect>
                        <path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V4a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h9a2 2 0 0 1 2 2v1"></path>
                    </svg>
                    <span id="copy-text" style="display: none;"></span>
                </button>
                <button id="open-btn" title="Open in Native App">
                    <svg id="open-icon" width="18" height="18" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" 
                        stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round">
                        <path d="M18 13v6a2 2 0 0 1-2 2H5a2 2 0 0 1-2-2V8a2 2 0 0 1 2-2h6"></path>
                        <polyline points="15 3 21 3 21 9"></polyline>
                        <line x1="10" y1="14" x2="21" y2="3"></line>
                    </svg>
                    <span id="open-text" style="display: none;"></span>
                </button>
            </div>
        </nav>

        <div class="content-wrapper" id="content-wrapper">
            <div id="app">
                <div class="skeleton" style="height: 36px; width: 40%; margin-bottom: 24px;"></div>
                <div class="skeleton" style="height: 16px; width: 100%; margin-bottom: 12px;"></div>
                <div class="skeleton" style="height: 16px; width: 95%; margin-bottom: 12px;"></div>
                <div class="skeleton" style="height: 16px; width: 90%; margin-bottom: 24px;"></div>
                <div class="skeleton" style="height: 16px; width: 80%; margin-bottom: 12px;"></div>
            </div>
            <div class="fade-overlay" id="fade-overlay"></div>
        </div>

        <div class="toggle-container" id="toggle-container">
            <button id="toggle-btn">
                <span id="toggle-text">Show More</span>
                <svg id="toggle-icon" width="16" height="16" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor"
                    stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" style="transform: rotate(0deg);">
                    <polyline points="6 9 12 15 18 9"></polyline>
                </svg>
            </button>
        </div>
    </div>

    <script>
        const appDiv = document.getElementById('app');
        const INIT_ID = 1;
        let isExpanded = false; // START COLLAPSED BY DEFAULT!

        let rawMarkdownText = "";
        let currentFilePath = null;
        const navTitle = document.getElementById('nav-title');
        const copyBtn = document.getElementById('copy-btn');
        const copyIcon = document.getElementById('copy-icon');
        const copyText = document.getElementById('copy-text');
        const openBtn = document.getElementById('open-btn');
        const openText = document.getElementById('open-text');

        function fallbackCopyTextToClipboard(text) {
            return new Promise((resolve, reject) => {
                const textArea = document.createElement("textarea");
                textArea.value = text;
                textArea.style.top = "0";
                textArea.style.left = "0";
                textArea.style.position = "fixed";
                textArea.style.opacity = "0";

                document.body.appendChild(textArea);
                textArea.focus();
                textArea.select();

                try {
                    const successful = document.execCommand('copy');
                    if (successful) {
                        resolve();
                    } else {
                        reject(new Error("execCommand returned false"));
                    }
                } catch (err) {
                    reject(err);
                } finally {
                    document.body.removeChild(textArea);
                }
            });
        }

        copyBtn.addEventListener('click', async () => {
            try {
                if (navigator.clipboard && window.isSecureContext) {
                    try {
                        await navigator.clipboard.writeText(rawMarkdownText);
                    } catch (e) {
                        await fallbackCopyTextToClipboard(rawMarkdownText);
                    }
                } else {
                    await fallbackCopyTextToClipboard(rawMarkdownText);
                }

                const originalSvg = copyIcon.innerHTML;
                copyIcon.innerHTML = `<polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline>`;

                copyText.textContent = "Copied!";
                copyText.style.display = "inline";
                copyBtn.style.color = "#10b981";

                setTimeout(() => {
                    copyIcon.innerHTML = originalSvg;
                    copyText.textContent = "";
                    copyText.style.display = "none";
                    copyBtn.style.color = "";
                }, 2000);
            } catch (err) {
                console.error('Failed to copy text: ', err);
                copyText.textContent = "Failed";
                copyText.style.display = "inline";
                copyBtn.title = err.toString();

                setTimeout(() => {
                    copyText.textContent = "";
                    copyText.style.display = "none";
                    copyBtn.title = "Copy Markdown";
                }, 3000);
            }
        });

        openBtn.addEventListener('click', () => {
            if (!currentFilePath) return;

            const reqId = Date.now();

            window.parent.postMessage({
                jsonrpc: "2.0",
                id: reqId,
                method: "tools/call",
                params: {
                    name: "open_local_file",
                    arguments: {
                        file_path: currentFilePath
                    }
                }
            }, "*");

            openText.textContent = "Opening...";
            openText.style.display = "inline";
            
            setTimeout(() => {
                openText.style.display = "none";
                openText.textContent = "";
            }, 2000);
        });

        function escapeHtml(unsafe) {
            return (unsafe || "").toString()
                .replace(/&/g, "&")
                .replace(/</g, "<")
                .replace(/>/g, ">")
                .replace(/"/g, """)
                .replace(/'/g, "'");
        }

        function showError(title, message, details = null) {
            let html = `
            <div class="alert">
                <div class="alert-title">
                    <svg width="20" height="20" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><circle cx="12" cy="12" r="10"></circle><line x1="12" y1="8" x2="12" y2="12"></line><line x1="12" y1="16" x2="12.01" y2="16"></line></svg>
                    ${escapeHtml(title)}
                </div>
                <div class="alert-body">${escapeHtml(message)}</div>
            `;
            if (details) {
                html += `<div class="alert-details">${escapeHtml(details)}</div>`;
            }
            html += `</div>`;
            appDiv.innerHTML = html;
        }

        function checkCollapsible() {
            const wrapper = document.getElementById('content-wrapper');
            const toggleContainer = document.getElementById('toggle-container');
            const fadeOverlay = document.getElementById('fade-overlay');
            const toggleIcon = document.getElementById('toggle-icon');
            const toggleText = document.getElementById('toggle-text');

            // 1. If no markdown, we are showing the skeleton. Do not collapse!
            if (!rawMarkdownText) {
                toggleContainer.style.display = 'none';
                wrapper.style.maxHeight = 'none';
                wrapper.style.overflowY = 'visible';
                fadeOverlay.style.display = 'none';
                triggerResize();
                return;
            }

            // 2. Data is loaded
            const realHeight = appDiv.scrollHeight;
            const COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD = 300;
            const COLLAPSED_HEIGHT = 150;
            const EXPANDED_MAX_HEIGHT = 500;

            if (realHeight > COLLAPSE_THRESHOLD) {
                toggleContainer.style.display = 'flex';
                if (isExpanded) {
                    // Open view! Scroll inside max 500px window
                    wrapper.style.maxHeight = EXPANDED_MAX_HEIGHT + 'px';
                    wrapper.style.overflowY = 'auto';
                    fadeOverlay.style.display = 'none';
                    toggleIcon.style.transform = 'rotate(180deg)';
                    toggleText.textContent = 'Show Less';
                } else {
                    // Collapsed View
                    wrapper.style.maxHeight = COLLAPSED_HEIGHT + 'px';
                    wrapper.style.overflowY = 'hidden';
                    fadeOverlay.style.display = 'block';
                    toggleIcon.style.transform = 'rotate(0deg)';
                    toggleText.textContent = 'Show More';
                }
            } else {
                toggleContainer.style.display = 'none';
                wrapper.style.maxHeight = 'none';
                wrapper.style.overflowY = 'visible';
                fadeOverlay.style.display = 'none';
            }

            triggerResize();
        }

        document.getElementById('toggle-btn').addEventListener('click', () => {
            isExpanded = !isExpanded;
            checkCollapsible();
        });

        function triggerResize() {
            // Measure the exact unconstrained height of the outer card
            const card = document.querySelector('.adeu-card');
            const containerChrome = 34; // 16px padding top/bottom + 2px borders

            const requestedHeight = card.offsetHeight + containerChrome;

            window.parent.postMessage({
                jsonrpc: "2.0",
                method: "ui/notifications/size-changed",
                params: { height: Math.ceil(requestedHeight), width: 600 }
            }, "*");
        }

        window.addEventListener("message", (event) => {
            try {
                if (!event.data || event.data.jsonrpc !== "2.0") return;
                const msg = event.data;

                if (msg.id === INIT_ID) {
                    window.parent.postMessage({
                        jsonrpc: "2.0",
                        method: "ui/notifications/initialized",
                        params: {}
                    }, "*");
                    return;
                }

                if (msg.method === "ui/notifications/tool-result") {
                    if (window.__MARKED_ERROR || typeof marked === 'undefined') {
                        showError("Missing Dependency", "The Markdown renderer failed to load.", window.__MARKED_ERROR || "marked is undefined.");
                        return;
                    }

                    const result = msg.params;

                    // If the state is pending, or no markdown is returned, completely hide the UI
                    if (!result || !result.structuredContent || result.structuredContent.status === "pending" || !result.structuredContent.markdown) {
                        document.documentElement.style.display = 'none';
                        window.parent.postMessage({
                            jsonrpc: "2.0",
                            method: "ui/notifications/size-changed",
                            params: { height: 0, width: 0 }
                        }, "*");
                        return;
                    }

                    // Render Markdown
                    rawMarkdownText = result.structuredContent.markdown;
                    if (result.structuredContent.file_path) {
                        currentFilePath = result.structuredContent.file_path;
                        openBtn.style.display = 'flex';
                    } else {
                        currentFilePath = null;
                        openBtn.style.display = 'none';
                    }
                    appDiv.innerHTML = marked.parse(rawMarkdownText);
                    copyBtn.style.display = 'flex';

                    if (result.structuredContent.title) {
                        navTitle.textContent = result.structuredContent.title;
                        navTitle.title = result.structuredContent.title;
                    }
                }
            } catch (error) {
                showError("Rendering Engine Error", "An unexpected error occurred.", error.toString() + "\n" + error.stack);
            }
        });

        const observer = new ResizeObserver(() => {
            checkCollapsible();
        });
        observer.observe(appDiv);

        window.parent.postMessage({
            jsonrpc: "2.0",
            id: INIT_ID,
            method: "ui/initialize",
            params: {
                appInfo: { name: "Adeu Markdown UI", version: "1.1.0" },
                appCapabilities: {},
                protocolVersion: "2025-11-21"
            }
        }, "*");
    </script>
</body>

</html>

blocks.text:
I have now completed my review of all key documents. Here's my structured read for you:

---

## **TOP-3 WALK-AWAY ISSUES**

**1. Foreground IP Assignment — Absolute to Buyer (Clause 9.2)**

Pemberton demands complete assignment of all Foreground IP created during the contract — "including any modifications, improvements, derivatives, or jointly developed materials" — with full title guarantee and future rights. For a 3-year MRO supply framework, this is disproportionate. You are likely to develop supply logistics, quality-control methodologies, and process optimization tied to Pemberton's specific operations. Assigning these absolutely leaves you with no ability to improve your own supply chain or apply learnings across your other customers. This needs a carve-out: joint ownership of Foreground IP, or a non-exclusive perpetual licence-back to you. **Non-negotiable.**

**2. Unilateral Termination for Convenience by Buyer Only (Clause 18.1)**

Pemberton can terminate at any time on 30 days' notice with capped early-termination charges (5% of WIP). You have no reciprocal right. For a 3-year framework worth ~£18.4m annual spend (54m+ over term), this converts the deal into a month-to-month rolling arrangement from Pemberton's perspective. Combined with the £100k cap on Buyer liability (clause 11.2), you bear all the downside: stranded inventory, supplier commitments, dedicated staffing. Either you get a reciprocal termination right, or you get material protection: six-month wind-down notice, demobilisation costs (staffing rundown, inventory liquidation), and guaranteed minimum volume for quarters 1–2. **This is your primary leverage point.**

**3. Uncapped Indemnity + Broad Scope (Clause 10.1–10.2)**

Supplier indemnifies Buyer for breach, negligence, defect, IP infringement, **and "any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation"** — all uncapped and surviving termination. The indemnity sits outside the liability cap (clause 11.1), meaning it's your only exposure stream with no ceiling. The regulatory breach head is particularly dangerous: it captures Bribery Act, Modern Slavery, GDPR, sanctions compliance — any of which could trigger six-figure claims or withdrawal from the supply agreement without limit. Rework to: mutual indemnities; cap the IP infringement indemnity at 12-month fees (higher than general cap but reasonable for IP); exclude regulatory indemnity or cap it at the same ceiling as general liability. **Critical.**

---

## **TOP-5 HIGH-PRIORITY PUSH-BACK ITEMS**

**1. Data Processing Annex — "Updated from Time to Time" (Clause 15.1)**

The DPA is incorporated by reference and defined as "from time to time," meaning Pemberton can change the terms unilaterally post-signature without your consent. This is non-standard and creates ongoing compliance risk. Push for: fixed DPA version attached as Appendix with change procedure requiring mutual consent (or 60-day notice + right to terminate if materially adverse). **Non-negotiable for data-handling contracts.**

**2. Most-Favoured-Customer Clause (Pricing Schedule § 7 + MSA 5.4)**

You must match "any verified third-party offer for substantially comparable Goods." Combined with Pemberton's right to benchmark prices at any time (5.4) and audit pricing (16.1), this creates a permanent compliance burden and margin compression risk. MFC clauses are standard in procurement but combined with open-book costing (§ 6) on your Top 50 SKUs, Pemberton has full visibility of your margins and your competitors' offers. **Push back:** Limit MFC to a defined cohort of comparable customers (e.g., "similarly sized manufacturing operations in the automotive and industrial sectors") and require written notice + 30-day response window before the improvement is due. Without scope narrowing, your margins will erode over the term.

**3. Payment Terms — 90 Days (MSA 6.2)**

90 days from receipt is aggressive for an MRO supplier funding inventory and working capital. On £18.4m annual spend that's ~£4.6m in float at any time. Standard is 30–60 days for indirect spend. **Push:** 60 days, or if 90 is immovable, negotiate a 2% early-payment discount for payment by day 30 or day 45 to offset working-capital cost.

**4. Open-Book Costing + Margin Compression (Pricing Schedule § 6)**

Buyer reserves the right to "require margin compression where Supplier margin exceeds twelve percent (12%)" on your top 50 SKUs. This is effectively price reduction at will once your costs are transparent. Twelve percent is aggressive for indirect MRO: typical margins are 15–25% depending on category and volume. **Push:** (a) define the 50 SKUs jointly, with quarterly review; (b) raise the threshold to 18% for most categories, 20% for high-touch categories (capital spares); (c) carve out costs related to compliance (cyber, modern slavery, environmental audits) from the margin calculation; (d) include a "no downward adjustment" clause — margins can only be reviewed if Buyer costs fall, not as a revenue-optimization mechanism.

**5. Audit Rights — Scope and Survival (Clause 16)**

Five business days' notice is workable, but "records, premises, systems and personnel" is broad, and 6-year survival post-termination is standard. **However**, combined with the compliance audit rights in the Compliance Annex and Buyer's right to use third-party auditors at your cost, this creates overlapping audit exposure. Push for: (a) audit notice increased to 10 business days for compliance audits; (b) cost-sharing if Buyer uses third-party auditors (you bear Supplier's own costs, Buyer bears auditor fees); (c) survival capped at 4 years post-termination (reasonable for MRO documentation); (d) cap frequency: maximum two audits per year unless a specific risk event has triggered investigation.

---

## **ACCEPTABLE / NICE-TO-HAVE ITEMS**

- **Term and extensions (3.1–3.2):** 3 years + 2-year extension on mutual agreement is standard. Accept.
- **Order cancellation (4.3):** 15% capped reimbursement for pre-despatch cancellations is reasonable for MRO.
- **Price escalation cap (5.2):** Prices firm for Year 1; thereafter capped at lower of CPI or 2.5%. Tight but accept — it avoids inflation pass-through and gives you visibility for cost management.
- **Risk and title (7.1–7.2):** DDP Incoterms 2020 to receiving site; risk passes on signed acceptance; title on payment. Standard for distribution.
- **Quality warranty (8.3):** 24-month defect warranty is reasonable for industrial goods.
- **Insurance (12.1):** £10m product liability is high for indirect MRO but not disqualifying; push if you're a smaller supplier. £5m professional indemnity is reasonable.
- **Force majeure carve-out (17.2):** Only Buyer can terminate if FM event exceeds 30 days — asymmetric but standard in procurement. Acceptable if covered by your own FM reserves.
- **Confidentiality (14):** Standard mutual confidentiality with indefinite tail — acceptable.
- **Compliance annex:** Modern Slavery, Bribery Act, sanctions, environmental (ISO 14001), annual ESG attestation at director level. All standard for large procurement in the UK/EU. Make sure you can attest accurately; if not, flag items in attestation template now and propose remediation timelines in the bid. **Do not overstate capability.**

---

## **COMMERCIALLY MATERIAL ISSUES TO PRICE**

1. **Working capital float:** 90-day payment terms + £18.4m annual spend = ~£4.6m in financed inventory. Factor this into your margin or push for shortened terms.

2. **Compliance overhead:** Modern Slavery due diligence, Bribery Act self-assessment, cyber (Cyber Essentials Plus), annual ESG attestation (including Scope 3 emissions + SBTi target validation). Budget for annual compliance cost and fold into pricing.

3. **SLA penalties:** Aggressive OTIF targets (94% per SLA). Miss 3 consecutive months = material breach. Monthly penalties are 1–3% of monthly spend depending on miss severity. Ensure your supply chain can hit these or price a risk premium.

4. **Audit and data-processing costs:** Two+ audits per year + data-processor compliance + third-party audit cost absorption. Budget for 0.5–1% of contract value annually for compliance/audit infrastructure.

---

## **PRECEDENCE / BATTLE OF FORMS ALERT**

The MSA states (clause 1.3 & 20.1) that **GTCs prevail over all documents** — including the MSA itself. This is aggressive form language. Pemberton's GTCs (Document 05) are in PDF and difficult to read (OCR degradation), but the RFQ invitation letter confirms: GTCs > MSA > SLA > Pricing Schedule > Orders. **This means Pemberton's standing terms override your negotiated MSA changes.** Request a clean read of the GTCs. If they contradict the MSA on material points (liability, termination, IP), you need explicit carve-out language in the signed MSA or you're effectively bidding blind.

---

## **NEXT STEPS**

1. **Confirm scope:** Are you the sole source or one of two preferred suppliers?
2. **Flag precedence:** Request clean redacted version of GTCs (Document 05) from Pemberton; confirm which terms will be non-negotiable and which you can move.
3. **Redline strategy:** I recommend marking up the MSA to address walk-aways #1–3 first (IP assignment, termination reciprocity, indemnity cap). Use "Commercial Issue" language in track changes rather than binary rejects — you're signalling negotiation posture, not refusal.
4. **Pricing calibration:** Once walk-away terms are resolved, price the remaining items (90-day terms, margin compression risk, compliance burden). Build a pricelist that reflects the true cost of compliance and working capital.

I can draft a redlined MSA with specific language if you want to move to markup now, or walk through the pricing implications once you've landed on the deal structure. What's your read on Pemberton's flexibility here?
What's our exposure if their GTCs (Document 05) override the MSA on indemnities? Worst case, what are we signing up to?
Let me pull Document 05 to see what their GTCs actually say on indemnities and liabilities.

arguments.content:
# Pemberton RFQ Review — Exposure Analysis

## Active Task
- Quantifying exposure if GTCs override MSA on indemnities and liability
- Comparing MSA language vs. GTCs language on key risk provisions

## Key Findings So Far

### MSA Structure (Documents 2):
- Uncapped indemnity to Buyer (Clause 10.1–10.2) covering breach, negligence, defect, IP infringement, **"any breach...of any applicable law"**
- Supplier liability capped at 12 months fees (Clause 11.1)
- Buyer liability capped at £100k (Clause 11.2)
- Indemnity survives termination and sits outside the cap
- Carve-out from liability cap: IP infringement, confidentiality, clauses 13/15/16 (compliance/data)

### GTCs Battle of Forms (Document 5):
- GTCs prevail over all documents unless expressly agreed otherwise in writing by director-level Buyer rep
- Need to extract exact liability cap language and indemnity scope

## TODO
- [ ] Map exact GTCs liability cap (appears to be £100k or Order value ceiling per GTCs § on Liability)
- [ ] Cross-reference GTCs indemnity scope vs. MSA indemnity scope
- [ ] Calculate worst-case exposure: framework value £18.4m annual, 3 years = £55.2m
- [ ] Identify if GTCs indemnity is broader or narrower than MSA
- [ ] Prepare risk memo on precedence exposure
- [ ] Develop redline strategy to lock down MSA terms or add GTC carve-outs

blocks.text:
Reading the OCR carefully, here's what the GTCs say on liability and indemnity. The text is degraded but the key language is recoverable:

---

## **GTCs LIABILITY & INDEMNITY ANALYSIS**

### **From GTCs, Page 4 — "Remedies" Section:**

**Supplier's Liability for Breach — UNCAPPED except in specific carve-outs:**

> "Suppliers liability for any breach is **unlimited in respect of** (i) death or personal injury, (ii) fraud, (iii) breach of confidentiality, (iv) **Foreground IP infringement**, (v) loss of or damage to tangible property at a Buyer site, and **(vi) Suppliers indemnity obligations**."

Then — this is critical — there's a **general cap:**

> "Subject to clause [X], Suppliers aggregate liability under any Order is capped at the **greater of (i) £1m or (ii) the value of that Order.**"

### **From GTCs, Page 4 — "Intellectual Property" Section:**

> "**Any Foreground IP arising in the performance of any Order vests in Buyer absolutely on creation.** Supplier hereby assigns to Buyer with full title guarantee all Foreground IP… **Supplier grants Buyer a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, sublicensable licence over any Background IP embedded in any Goods or Services or required to exploit any Foreground IP.**"

### **From GTCs, Page 4 — "Suppliers Indemnity Obligations" Section:**

> "Supplier shall **indemnify Buyer** in respect of any claim that the Goods, Services, or any Foreground IP **infringe any third-party IP Right.**"

This is narrower than the MSA (which covers breach of law, negligence, defect broadly).

---

## **WORST-CASE EXPOSURE — BATTLE OF FORMS SCENARIO**

Now the collision: **If GTCs prevail over the MSA** (which is what the precedence clauses state), here's what you're signing:

| Risk Category | MSA Language | GTCs Language | **Which Prevails?** | Exposure |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **General Liability Cap** | 12 months fees (~£15.3m) | Greater of £1m or Order value | **GTCs** | Capped at ~£1m per order, but multiple concurrent orders = aggregate exposure still high |
| **Indemnity Scope** | Uncapped; covers breach, negligence, defect, IP, "any breach of applicable law" | Limited to IP infringement claims only | **GTCs** (narrower, surprisingly) | Actually better — GTCs indemnity is narrower |
| **Indemnity Cap** | Sits outside liability cap; survives termination | Sits outside general cap (clause says "unlimited") | **GTCs** | Still unlimited, but only for IP claims under GTCs |
| **Carve-outs from Cap** | IP, confidentiality, compliance (13/15/16) | Death/PI, fraud, confidentiality, IP, tangible property damage, indemnity | **GTCs** | GTCs add tangible property damage as unlimited; this is procurement-specific |
| **Foreground IP Ownership** | Absolute assignment to Buyer | Absolute assignment to Buyer | **Same** | No difference |
| **Background IP Licence** | Perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, sublicensable | Same | **Same** | No difference |

---

## **THE REAL PROBLEM — READING THE COLLISION**

The GTCs say Supplier's "aggregate liability...is capped at the greater of £1m or the value of that Order" — **but then carve out six categories as unlimited**. Here's the issue:

**If you're supplying a £5m framework over 3 years at roughly £1.5–2m per annum:**

- **Per-order cap:** Most individual purchase orders might be £50k–£500k. So the £1m cap applies to each small order.
- **BUT** the six carve-outs are uncapped. If any of these apply — especially **(vi) Supplier's indemnity obligations** — you're back to unlimited exposure.
- **Circular reference:** GTCs say indemnity obligations are unlimited. But what are those obligations? The GTCs only name IP infringement. **However,** the MSA (which GTCs prevail over) says indemnity covers "any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation."

**If Pemberton's lawyer argues:** "GTCs prevail, so we apply the GTCs indemnity scope (IP only) but the GTCs say indemnity obligations are unlimited carve-outs from the cap, so your IP indemnity is uncapped" — you're exposed to the full value of any third-party IP claim, no matter how large.

---

## **QUANTIFIED WORST-CASE**

**Scenario: Third-party patent claim against Pemberton alleging a component you supplied infringes a patent.**

| Factor | Calculation | Exposure |
|---|---|---|
| **Framework value (3 years)** | £18.4m annual × 3 = £55.2m | Baseline |
| **Indemnity scope under GTCs** | IP infringement only | Narrower than MSA (good) |
| **Indemnity cap under GTCs** | "Unlimited" per carve-out language | **No dollar limit** |
| **Third-party claim value** | Assume patent damages: injunction + lost profits + damages | Could be £5m–£50m+ depending on industry |
| **Your exposure** | Indemnify Buyer for full amount, no cap | **£5m–£50m+ (or whatever the claim is)** |
| **Survival** | MSA says indemnity survives termination; GTCs silent | Likely survives; you could face claims years post-termination |

**This is the walk-away issue #3 from my first read — but now amplified by GTCs precedence.**

---

## **SECONDARY EXPOSURE — REGULATORY INDEMNITY**

The MSA Clause 10.1 says Supplier indemnifies Buyer for "any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation."

**Does this survive if GTCs prevail?** 

The GTCs limit the indemnity to "IP infringement" but don't expressly exclude regulatory claims. A sharp Pemberton lawyer could argue:
- "GTCs indemnity is IP-only (narrower)."
- "But the MSA regulatory indemnity is a separate **warranty** that survives as a carve-out from the liability cap."
- "And compliance indemnity is carved out from the cap under MSA 11.3(iii)."

**Result:** You could be reading two indemnities:
1. GTCs IP indemnity (uncapped per carve-out)
2. MSA regulatory indemnity (uncapped, survives per MSA 10.2)

---

## **WHAT YOU'RE ACTUALLY SIGNING IF GTCs PREVAIL**

Taking the most aggressive reading:

1. **Per-order liability cap: £1m or order value, whichever is greater.**
   - This is better than the MSA (12 months fees = ~£15m).

2. **BUT six carve-outs are unlimited:**
   - **(vi) Suppliers indemnity obligations** — interpreted as: all indemnity obligations (IP under GTCs, but potentially regulatory under MSA if read together).

3. **IP Indemnity: Uncapped, survives termination, covers all third-party IP claims.**
   - A single patent suit could expose you to £5m–£50m+.

4. **Regulatory indemnity: Likely uncapped via the "compliance" carve-out (MSA 11.3(iii)).**
   - Bribery Act, Modern Slavery, GDPR, sanctions — any of these could trigger claims.

5. **Tangible property damage: Uncapped.**
   - If your goods damage Pemberton's plant or equipment, no cap.

6. **Precedence:** GTCs prevail unless Buyer director-level expressly agrees otherwise in writing.
   - You almost certainly won't get director-level sign-off on exceptions.

---

## **COMPARISON: MSA-ONLY vs. GTCs-PREVAILING**

| Scenario | General Liability Cap | Indemnity Cap | IP Infringement | Regulatory Breach | Total Exposure |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MSA Alone** | 12 months fees (~£15m) | Uncapped but outside cap | Uncapped, outside cap | Uncapped, outside cap | Indemnity unlimited; can reach £20m–£50m+ |
| **GTCs Prevail** | Greater of £1m or Order value | Uncapped (carve-out) | Uncapped | Potentially uncapped (if read with MSA) | Indemnity unlimited; similar or worse |

**Net:** Surprisingly similar. The GTCs per-order cap (£1m) sounds better, but the carve-outs swallow it.

---

## **YOUR NEGOTIATION POSTURE**

**Immediate action:** Request Pemberton confirm in writing that the MSA prevails over the GTCs on:
1. Liability cap (12 months fees, not £1m per order)
2. Indemnity scope (limited to IP + regulatory compliance as defined)
3. Indemnity cap (same as general liability, not outside cap)

**If they refuse:** You need to redline both documents to create explicit precedence and carve-outs. Otherwise, you're signing a contract where a single IP or compliance claim can bankrupt you.

**Pricing adjustment:** If you can't lock down the indemnity, price in a 2–3% risk premium to cover the unhedged tail risk (IP litigation, regulatory defense costs, reputational damage).

Does this change your appetite for the deal? And do you want me to draft a precedence clause that clarifies which document wins on indemnity/liability?
Walk me through your top-3 redline targets and why each matters. Imagine I'm pitching this to the commercial sponsor in 5 minutes.

I'll lay out the three redlines in the order you pitch them — commercial impact first, legal risk second, negotiation feasibility third.

---

## **REDLINE #1: TERMINATE BUYER'S UNILATERAL TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE**

**The Problem:**
Clause 18.1 lets Pemberton kill the contract anytime on 30 days' notice with only 5% of work-in-progress reimbursed. You have no reciprocal right. On an £18.4m annual framework, that's ~£4.6m in float at any time. If Pemberton walks, you're left with stranded inventory, locked-in supplier commitments, and dedicated staff with no revenue to cover them.

**Why It Matters — Commercial:**
- **Cash flow cliff.** You're financing their 90-day payment terms (£4.6m float) plus carrying safety stock across 1,247 SKUs. If they terminate Month 14, you've sunk working capital with no recovery.
- **Supplier lock-in.** You'll likely need to commit capacity with your upstream suppliers (the manufacturers you buy from) to guarantee 94% OTIF (on-time-in-full). If Pemberton terminates early, your suppliers won't take back the committed capacity — you eat the cost.
- **Staffing cost.** MRO distribution requires regional logistics teams, customer service staff, and IT infrastructure. Dedicated to Pemberton, these are stranded costs if the deal ends.

**Why It Matters — Legal:**
If GTCs prevail, Pemberton has even more termination triggers: "any breach" (undefined), insolvency, change of control, **or "any act that in Buyer's reasonable opinion brings Buyer into disrepute."** That last one is subjective and unilateral.

**What You're Asking For:**
Make termination reciprocal. Either:
- **Option A (preferred):** Both parties can terminate for convenience on 90 days' notice. On termination, Supplier gets: (i) full reimbursement of committed supplier costs (not capped at 5%); (ii) wind-down costs (staffing rundown over 60 days); (iii) inventory liquidation costs (sell-off at cost, Buyer covers delta to market).
- **Option B (fallback):** Pemberton can terminate unilaterally, but only after Year 2. Years 1–2 are locked. Termination costs include full committed supplier costs + demobilisation (not 5% of WIP).

**Negotiation Reality:**
Pemberton won't give reciprocal termination — they're a large buyer and value the optionality. But they will likely agree to lock Year 1 (sometimes Year 1–2) and expand the termination fee to cover demonstrable committed costs (not just WIP). This is **moderately achievable** if you quantify the supplier commitments in your bid response.

**Why Pitch This First:**
It's the biggest cash flow risk and directly affects pricing. Your CFO cares about this more than they care about IP assignment or regulatory indemnity. And it's commercial, not legal—easier for the business to understand.

---

## **REDLINE #2: CAP AND NARROW THE INDEMNITY**

**The Problem:**
MSA Clause 10 says you indemnify Pemberton for "any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation" — **uncapped and surviving termination**. GTCs say indemnity carve-outs are unlimited. One Modern Slavery Act investigation, one GDPR enforcement action, one sanctions screening miss, and Pemberton points to this clause and demands you cover their legal defense, fines, and reputational costs. No cap.

**Why It Matters — Commercial:**
- **Regulatory liability is not insurable.** Your standard commercial general liability (CGL) policy excludes regulatory fines and penalties. Your Professional Indemnity policy excludes fines. You're **personally on the hook** for a regulatory claim.
- **Pemberton's defense costs dwarf yours.** If Pemberton is investigated by the UK Serious Fraud Office (SFO) for Bribery Act violations in their supply chain, their defense costs are £2m–£5m. They turn around and demand you indemnify them because "your supplier failed Modern Slavery due diligence." You can't absorb that.
- **Regulatory indemnity is a cliff.** There's no upper bound. If GDPR enforcement action reaches £10m fine, you owe it — not £100k, not £1m, **all of it**.

**Why It Matters — Legal:**
The MSA and GTCs battle creates ambiguity. Does "breach of applicable law" include:
- Supplier's own regulatory failures (e.g., you fail to conduct Modern Slavery due diligence on a sub-supplier)? **Yes.**
- Buyer's regulatory failures that touch your goods (e.g., Buyer exports your goods to a sanctioned jurisdiction without checking with you)? **Unclear — Pemberton will argue yes.**
- Buyer's failure to notify you of a regulatory investigation affecting the goods? **No, but you still indemnify them for all investigation costs.**

**What You're Asking For:**
Narrow and cap the indemnity:

> "Supplier shall indemnify Buyer for claims arising from **(i) Supplier's material breach of Supplier's own compliance obligations set out in Compliance Annex (Modern Slavery, Bribery Act, sanctions screening, data protection)**; and **(ii) any third-party IP infringement claim relating to the Goods.** 
>
> Supplier's indemnity liability shall not exceed the aggregate of fees paid in the 12 months preceding the claim. Indemnity shall not apply to (a) Buyer's regulatory failures or omissions; (b) Buyer's failure to notify Supplier of a regulatory investigation; or (c) regulatory action arising from Buyer's use or resale of the Goods outside the scope authorized by Supplier."

This carves out Buyer's own failures and caps exposure at annual fees (~£15m, not unlimited).

**Negotiation Reality:**
Pemberton will push back hard. They'll say "we need protection from our suppliers' regulatory failures." **You concede the point** but negotiate the cap. Offer: "We'll indemnify you for our own compliance failures, capped at annual fees. For anything beyond that, you have audit rights and can terminate." This is **moderately achievable** because Pemberton has audit rights anyway (Compliance Annex + Audit Rights clause) — they can police you if they're worried.

**Why Pitch This Second:**
It's the second-biggest walk-away. If you can't cap this, you can't price the deal. And it flows logically from Redline #1: "We're locking in commitment for you; in return, you cap our tail risk."

---

## **REDLINE #3: FIX PRECEDENCE — MSA PREVAILS ON LIABILITY/INDEMNITY**

**The Problem:**
MSA 1.3 and 20.1 say **GTCs prevail over the MSA unless Buyer director-level agrees otherwise.** You'll never get that sign-off. So de facto, GTCs prevail. GTCs liability cap is £1m per order (or order value), which sounds tighter than the MSA's 12-month-fees cap. But GTCs carve out "Supplier indemnity obligations" as unlimited. If a patent suit lands, you're uncapped. If a regulatory claim lands, you're uncapped. **You have no idea which version of the indemnity applies — the MSA version (broad) or the GTCs version (narrow).**

**Why It Matters — Commercial:**
- **Pricing uncertainty.** You can't quote pricing if you don't know which contract governs. Is your liability £1m per order or £15m? This changes your risk premium by 10–15%.
- **Claims disputes.** Post-incident, Pemberton will cherry-pick the version that favors them. IP claim? GTCs say indemnity is unlimited. Regulatory claim? MSA says indemnity covers "any breach of law." You're litigating precedence while defending the claim itself.

**Why It Matters — Legal:**
Under English law (choice of law in MSA 19.1), if two contracts conflict, the court looks at the order of precedence the parties intended. If the precedence clause itself is ambiguous (GTCs prevail "unless expressly agreed otherwise"), a court might:
- Apply parol evidence to determine what you *actually* agreed to.
- Interpret the ambiguity against the drafter (Pemberton — they drafted the precedence language).
- Find the entire precedence void and apply the UCTA 1977 reasonableness test.

You don't want to litigate this. **You want clarity at signature.**

**What You're Asking For:**
Replace MSA 20.1 and 1.3 with explicit precedence:

> "**Order of Precedence.** In the event of conflict between the documents in this pack, they shall rank in the following order: **(1) MSA (this document); (2) SLA (Document 04); (3) Pricing Schedule (Document 03); (4) GTCs (Document 05); (5) Orders.**
>
> **Notwithstanding clause 1.3, on the topics of liability, indemnity, and termination, the MSA shall prevail over the GTCs.**"

This makes clear: MSA governs liability/indemnity, not GTCs. If there's a conflict (e.g., MSA says "12 months fees," GTCs say "£1m"), MSA wins.

**Negotiation Reality:**
Pemberton will push back because their GTCs are their standard form. They'll say "our GTCs are our policy; we can't carve them out." **Your counter:** "We're not asking you to change your GTCs — we're just saying that for this 3-year, £55m framework, the negotiated MSA terms on liability and indemnity take precedence. Your GTCs still apply to everything else (delivery, warranty, compliance, etc.)." This is **highly achievable** because you're not asking them to change their template — just applying standard precedence logic.

**Why Pitch This Third:**
It's the "lock it down" move. It doesn't change the dollars, but it removes ambiguity. By the time you pitch this, you've already negotiated Redlines #1 and #2 (the cash-impacting ones). Redline #3 is the lawyer's version of "make sure we know which rules we're playing by." The commercial sponsor will understand it as risk de-risking, not deal-blocking.

---

## **THE 5-MINUTE PITCH TO THE CFO / SPONSOR**

> "We have three fixes to make this deal work.
>
> **First: Lock the first year.** Pemberton can terminate anytime on 30 days' notice right now. That leaves us holding £4.6 million in financed inventory and committed supplier capacity with no revenue cover. We need Year 1 locked, and termination fees that cover actual supplier commitments — not just 5% of work-in-progress. This changes the pricing model from month-to-month risk to year-locked commitment. 
>
> **Second: Cap the regulatory indemnity.** Right now we're on the hook for unlimited indemnity if Pemberton gets investigated by the SFO or hit with a GDPR fine, and we can't price or insure that tail. We're asking: we indemnify you for our own compliance failures, but capped at annual fees. You already have audit rights to police us, so you're protected. This is the difference between a 3% risk premium and an 8% risk premium on this deal.
>
> **Third: Fix the contract language.** Their GTCs and our MSA conflict on liability. Depending on how a lawyer reads it, we're either capped at £1m per order or uncapped on indemnity claims. We're not changing their form — we're just making clear that on liability and indemnity, the negotiated MSA terms apply. This removes a claim dispute down the road.
>
> **Bottom line:** We can live with 90-day payment terms and aggressive SLA penalties if we lock Year 1, cap the regulatory tail, and clarify the liability language. That's the package."

---

## **WHY THIS ORDER MATTERS**

1. **Redline #1 (termination)** = cash flow (CFO understands immediately)
2. **Redline #2 (indemnity cap)** = pricing model (flows from cash flow; lets you quantify risk premium)
3. **Redline #3 (precedence)** = legal certainty (lawyer move; by now the sponsor trusts the reasoning)

Each one builds on the last. You're not asking for three separate carve-outs; you're building a narrative: "Lock our commitment, cap our tail risk, and clarify the rules."

Ready to draft the actual redline language for the MSA, or do you want to test these three points with Pemberton first via an ask list?
Anything to add given what they sent over after the first batch?

I don't have visibility into any follow-up documents from Pemberton yet. What did they send? 

If there's a second batch of materials in the matter folder, I can pull and analyze it — or if you want to paste/describe what came back, I can immediately flag how it changes the redline strategy.

Are we looking at:
- Revised MSA draft with their responses to opening asks?
- Clarifications on the GTCs?
- New compliance or SLA requirements?
- A response letter with commercial pushback?

What's the filename or general category?
Mark up the Master Supply Agreement (02-master-supply-agreement.docx) with our redline based on your analysis. Save the output to the outputs/ subfolder of this matter.

I'll execute the three-step redline doctrine now. Let me start by confirming the legal intent, then read the document carefully, plan the coordinated edits, apply them, and verify coherence.

**Step 1: Intent Statement**

We're addressing three walk-away items:
1. **Reciprocate termination for convenience** — shift from unilateral buyer right to mutual termination, with locked Year 1 and expanded early-termination compensation (committed supplier costs + demobilisation, not just 5% of WIP).
2. **Cap and narrow the indemnity** — limit regulatory indemnity to Supplier's own compliance failures (not Buyer's), cap all indemnity at 12-month fees, and exclude Buyer's omissions/failures.
3. **Fix precedence** — make MSA prevail over GTCs on liability, indemnity, and termination, removing the "director-level written agreement" carve-out that makes GTCs de facto supreme.

Now reading the document to identify every clause that interacts with these intents:

content.text:
> **File Path:** `/root/Documents/Oscar GC/Commercial/S32-821314 Sprint 30 — Pemberton RFQ pack + redline c09/02-master-supply-agreement.docx`

# Master Supply Agreement (Draft for Bidder Mark-Up)

Pemberton Engineering Ltd. — Indirect MRO Framework — 3-Year Term

**THIS MASTER SUPPLY AGREEMENT** is made on [DATE] 2027

**BETWEEN**

* **PEMBERTON ENGINEERING LTD.**, a company incorporated in England and Wales with registered number 03741892 whose registered office is at Unit 14, Trafford Park Industrial Estate, Manchester M17 1RH (“**Buyer**”); and

* **[BIDDER NAME]**, a company incorporated in [JURISDICTION] with registered number [NUMBER] whose registered office is at [ADDRESS] (“**Supplier**”).

(each a “**Party**” and together the “**Parties**”).

## 1. Definitions and Interpretation

1.1 In this Agreement, the following definitions apply:

* “**Affiliate**” means, in relation to a person, any entity that controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with that person.

* “**Background IP**” means Intellectual Property Rights owned or licensed by a Party as at the Effective Date.

* “**Confidential Information**” has the meaning given in clause 14.

* “**Effective Date**” means 1 January 2027.

* “**Foreground IP**” means Intellectual Property Rights created or developed in the performance of this Agreement, whether solely by a Party or jointly.

* “**Goods**” means the goods described in the Pricing Schedule, as may be amended from time to time.

* “**GTCs**” means Buyer’s General Terms and Conditions of Purchase as in force from time to time.

* “**Intellectual Property Rights**” or “**IPR**” means all patents, copyrights, design rights, trade marks, trade secrets and other intellectual or industrial property rights worldwide whether registered or unregistered.

* “**Order**” means a purchase order issued by Buyer for Goods.

* “**Services**” means the related services described in the Pricing Schedule or any Order.

* “**Term**” has the meaning given in clause 3.

1.2 Headings are for convenience only and do not affect interpretation.

1.3 Where any conflict arises between this Agreement and the GTCs, the **GTCs shall prevail**.

## 2. Appointment

2.1 Buyer appoints Supplier on a non-exclusive basis to supply the Goods and Services during the Term.

2.2 Buyer makes no commitment to any minimum volume or value of Orders. Forecasts provided by Buyer are non-binding indications only.

## 3. Term

3.1 This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of **three (3) years** unless terminated earlier in accordance with clause 18 (the “**Initial Term**”).

3.2 The Initial Term may be extended for a further period of up to two (2) years by mutual written agreement, such extension to be on the same terms as this Agreement unless the Parties agree otherwise in writing.

## 4. Orders

4.1 Buyer may issue Orders by any reasonable means including electronically. Each Order shall be deemed to incorporate this Agreement and the GTCs.

4.2 Supplier shall acknowledge each Order within two (2) Business Days. Failure to acknowledge within that period shall constitute acceptance of the Order on the terms set out therein.

4.3 Buyer may cancel or amend any Order at any time prior to despatch without liability to Supplier other than reimbursement of demonstrable direct costs reasonably incurred up to the date of cancellation, capped at fifteen percent (15%) of the Order value.

## 5. Pricing and Rebates

5.1 Prices are as set out in the Pricing Schedule and are exclusive of VAT.

5.2 Prices are firm for the first twelve (12) months of the Term. Thereafter Supplier may propose annual price adjustments capped at the lower of UK CPI and 2.5%. Buyer shall have absolute discretion to accept or reject any proposed adjustment.

5.3 Rebates shall accrue and be paid as set out in the Pricing Schedule.

5.4 Buyer reserves the right to benchmark prices at any time and to require Supplier to match any verified third-party offer for substantially comparable Goods.

## 6. Invoicing and Payment

6.1 Supplier shall invoice Buyer on a monthly basis in arrears.

6.2 Payment terms are **ninety (90) days** from receipt of a valid undisputed invoice.

6.3 Where any sum is disputed, payment of the disputed portion may be withheld pending resolution; the undisputed balance shall be paid in accordance with clause 6.2.

6.4 Interest on overdue undisputed sums shall accrue at the statutory rate under the Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998. The parties agree that this rate represents a substantial remedy under section 8 of that Act.

## 7. Delivery and Risk

7.1 Delivery shall be Incoterms 2020 DDP to the receiving site nominated in the Order.

7.2 Risk in the Goods passes to Buyer on signed acceptance at the receiving site. Title passes on payment in full in accordance with clause 6.

7.3 Supplier shall meet the service levels in the SLA. Failure to meet OTIF targets in any three (3) consecutive months shall constitute material breach for the purposes of clause 18.2.

## 8. Quality and Acceptance

8.1 Goods shall conform to the specification, applicable industry standards, and any sample provided.

8.2 Buyer may inspect and test Goods at any reasonable time. Non-conforming Goods may be rejected within thirty (30) days of delivery, at Supplier’s cost and risk.

8.3 Supplier warrants Goods against defects in materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months from acceptance.

## 9. Intellectual Property

9.1 Each Party retains ownership of its Background IP.

9.2 **All Foreground IP** — including any modifications, improvements, derivatives, or jointly developed materials arising in connection with this Agreement — **shall vest in Buyer absolutely on creation**. Supplier hereby assigns to Buyer with full title guarantee all such Foreground IP, including future rights.

9.3 Supplier shall, at Buyer’s cost, do all such acts and execute all such documents as Buyer may reasonably require to perfect Buyer’s title.

9.4 Supplier grants Buyer a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, worldwide, sublicensable licence to use Supplier’s Background IP to the extent embedded in or required to exploit any Goods or Foreground IP.

## 10. Indemnity

10.1 **Supplier shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless Buyer, its Affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees and agents** from and against any and all claims, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including reasonable legal fees on a full indemnity basis) arising out of or in connection with:

* any breach by Supplier of this Agreement;

* any negligent act or omission of Supplier or its personnel;

* any defect in the Goods or Services;

* any infringement or alleged infringement of any third-party Intellectual Property Right; and

* any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation.

10.2 The indemnities in this clause 10 are uncapped and survive termination.

## 11. Limitation of Liability

11.1 Subject always to clause 10 (which is uncapped), Supplier’s aggregate liability under or in connection with this Agreement and any Order, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), misrepresentation or otherwise, shall not exceed an amount equal to the fees paid or payable by Buyer to Supplier in the twelve (12) months preceding the event giving rise to the claim.

11.2 **Buyer’s aggregate liability** under or in connection with this Agreement, howsoever arising, **shall in no event exceed £100,000.**

11.3 Neither Party shall be liable for indirect, consequential or pure economic loss; provided that this exclusion shall not apply to (i) Supplier’s indemnity obligations under clause 10, (ii) breach of confidentiality, or (iii) breach of clauses 13, 15 or 16.

## 12. Insurance

12.1 Supplier shall maintain at its own cost throughout the Term and for six (6) years thereafter:

* public and product liability insurance of not less than £10 million per claim and in the aggregate;

* professional indemnity insurance of not less than £5 million per claim and in the aggregate; and

* employer’s liability insurance to the statutory minimum.

12.2 Supplier shall provide certificates of insurance on request and shall give Buyer thirty (30) days’ notice of any material change to its cover.

## 13. Compliance

13.1 Supplier shall comply with the Compliance and ESG Annex (Document 06), which forms part of this Agreement.

13.2 Supplier represents and warrants that:

* it complies with the Modern Slavery Act 2015 and maintains a current modern slavery statement;

* it complies with the Bribery Act 2010 and maintains adequate procedures within the meaning of section 7 of that Act;

* it complies with all applicable sanctions, export control and anti-money-laundering laws;

* neither it, its Affiliates, nor any of its personnel involved in performing this Agreement is a Sanctioned Person or located in a Sanctioned Jurisdiction; and

* it maintains and shall maintain an environmental management system certified to ISO 14001 or equivalent.

13.3 Supplier shall provide an annual ESG attestation in the form prescribed in the Compliance Annex, signed at director level.

## 14. Confidentiality

14.1 Each Party shall treat the other’s Confidential Information as confidential and use it only for the purposes of this Agreement.

14.2 The obligations of confidence in clause 14.1 shall continue indefinitely.

14.3 The confidentiality obligations shall not apply to information that is (i) in the public domain through no fault of the receiving Party; (ii) lawfully received from a third party without restriction; or (iii) required to be disclosed by law or regulatory authority.

## 15. Data Protection

15.1 The Parties shall comply with the data processing terms set out in the Data Processing Annex available on request from Buyer’s Data Protection Officer, which shall be deemed to be incorporated into this Agreement on its current terms from time to time.

## 16. Audit Rights

16.1 Buyer may, on giving five (5) Business Days’ written notice, audit Supplier’s records, premises, systems and personnel insofar as they relate to Supplier’s performance of this Agreement, compliance with clause 13, or pricing under clause 5.

16.2 Audits may be conducted by Buyer or by a third-party auditor of Buyer’s choosing. Supplier shall provide reasonable cooperation and access at its own cost.

16.3 Audit rights survive termination for a period of six (6) years.

## 17. Force Majeure

17.1 Neither Party shall be liable for failure or delay in performance caused by an event beyond its reasonable control, provided that the affected Party gives prompt written notice and uses reasonable efforts to mitigate.

17.2 If a force majeure event continues for more than thirty (30) days, **Buyer** (but not Supplier) may terminate this Agreement on written notice without liability.

## 18. Termination

18.1 **Termination for Convenience.** Buyer may terminate this Agreement or any Order at any time **without cause** on **thirty (30) days’ written notice** to Supplier. No early-termination charges shall be payable other than reimbursement of demonstrable direct costs reasonably incurred up to the date of termination, capped at five percent (5%) of the value of work-in-progress on the termination date.

18.2 **Termination for Cause.** Either Party may terminate this Agreement on written notice if the other Party (i) commits a material breach that is not remediable, or is remediable but is not remedied within thirty (30) days of written notice; (ii) becomes insolvent; or (iii) undergoes a change of control to a competitor of Buyer.

18.3 On termination Supplier shall, at no charge, transfer to Buyer or a nominated replacement supplier all data, materials, and Foreground IP relating to the Goods and Services.

## 19. Governing Law and Jurisdiction

19.1 This Agreement and any dispute arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by **English law**.

19.2 The Parties submit to the **exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Manchester**, England.

## 20. General

20.1 **Order of precedence.** In the event of any conflict between (a) this Agreement, (b) the GTCs, (c) the SLA, (d) the Compliance Annex, or (e) any Order, the order of precedence shall be (b), (a), (d), (c), (e). For the avoidance of doubt, **the GTCs prevail over this Agreement**.

20.2 **Assignment.** Buyer may assign this Agreement or any rights under it to any Affiliate or to any successor in business. Supplier may not assign or subcontract without Buyer’s prior written consent.

20.3 **Variation.** No variation is effective unless in writing and signed by an authorised representative of each Party.

20.4 **Entire agreement.** This Agreement, together with the documents referred to in clause 20.1, constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties.

20.5 **Notices.** Notices shall be given in writing to the addresses set out at the head of this Agreement.

20.6 **Third-party rights.** A person who is not a Party has no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 to enforce any term of this Agreement, save that Affiliates of Buyer may enforce clauses 9, 10, 13, 14, 15 and 16.

**IN WITNESS WHEREOF** the Parties have executed this Agreement on the date set out at the head of this Agreement.

Signed for and on behalf of **Pemberton Engineering Ltd.**:

Name: Title: Date:

Signed for and on behalf of **[BIDDER NAME]**:

Name: Title: Date:
